content.plain_english: This week your child is reading the time on an analogue clock to the nearest five minutes — including o'clock, half past, quarter past and quarter to. They are also learning that there are 60 minutes in an hour and 24 hours in a day.
content.in_school: Children use small clocks with movable hands to make times the teacher calls out. They draw the hour and minute hands onto blank clock faces, and compare two times to work out which event happens first or how long something takes.
content.home_activity: Over the next few days, point to a kitchen clock or your watch and ask 'what time is it?' four times — once at quarter past something, once at half past, once at quarter to, and once on the hour. The variety matters more than the accuracy. End with: how long until tea time?
dinner_table_questions: What time do you usually go to bed? Show me on your fingers as a quarter to or half past.
How many minutes from quarter past 6 until half past 6?
If a film starts at 4 o'clock and ends at quarter past 5, how long is it?

key_vocabulary.definition: A unit of time. There are 24 hours in one day and 60 minutes in one hour.
A smaller unit of time. There are 60 minutes in one hour.
An exact hour, when the minute hand points to 12. For example, 3 o'clock.
Thirty minutes after the hour. The minute hand points to 6. For example, half past 4.
Quarter past is 15 minutes after the hour (minute hand on 3). Quarter to is 15 minutes before the next hour (minute hand on 9).

example_questions: Draw the hands on a clock face to show quarter past 7.
What is the time 30 minutes after half past 2?
How many minutes are there in 2 hours?
content.times_table_tip: Reading minutes past the hour on an analogue clock means counting in 5s around the face — each number on the clock is 5 minutes further on. The 5 times table is the secret weapon.
